text: Both of these programs have short execution times, typically from a few to 20 seconds on the IBM/360-75 as compared with the five or more minutes required for the more accurate finite-difference method.

However, in a test example involving a hypothetical vessel of glass containing a large internal (fission product) heat source with a specific power density of 0.2 cal sec $^{-1}$ cm $^{-3}$ (i.e., 80,910 Btu hr $^{-1}$ ft $^{-3}$ ), the temperature difference between the wall and the center of a 6-in.-diam by 6.25-ft-long cylinder was overestimated by $36^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ (an error of only about $10\%$ , as compared with the "exact" value that is obtained by solving finite-difference equations and compensating for the reduction in the heat-source strength as the density decreases with increasing temperature).

On the basis of safety and economic studies, it appears rather probable that $\text{high-}^{1-4}$ and intermediate-level radioactive wastes which accumulate from the processing of nuclear reactor fuels will be converted to solids for permanent storage at some time - from 30 days to 30 years - after removal of the fuel from the reactor.
\frac {1}{r} \frac {\partial}{\partial r} \left[ K r \frac {\partial T}{\partial r} \right] + \frac {\partial}{\partial z} \left[ K \frac {\partial T}{\partial z} \right] + A = 0, \tag {1}

\begin{array}{l} K = \text {t e m p e r a t u r e - d e p e n d e n t} \\ T = \text {t e m p e r a t u r e}, ^ {\circ} C, \\ r = \text {r a d i a l} \\ z = \text {v e r t i c a l v a r i a b l e}, \mathrm {c m}, \\ \begin{array}{l} A = \text {s u m o f (f i s s i o n p r o d u c t) p o w e r d e n s i t i e s (i . e . , a b s o r b e d p o w e r d e n s i t y)}, \\ \text {c a l s e c} ^ {- 1} \text {c m} ^ {- 3}. \end{array} \\ \end{array}
(1) can be obtained only by use of finite-difference methods.

For example, the effect of temperature on the thermal conductivity of materials of interest is on the order of (50 to 200) $\times 10^{-6} \circ C^{-1}$ , while the coefficient of volume expansion is in the range (1 to 5) $\times 10^{-6} \circ C^{-1}$ .
(1) in terms of temperature as a function of spatial location within a cylinder of arbitrary length/diameter ratio.
(1) in terms of a dimensionless temperature, $\mathbf{v}$ , defined as
v \equiv (T - T _ {o}) / T _ {o}, \tag {2}
where $T_{0}$ is a convenient reference temperature.
In this report, $T_{0}$ was chosen as temperature of the surface of the cylinder.

\frac {1}{r} \frac {\partial}{\partial r} \left[ K r \frac {\partial v}{\partial r} \right] + \frac {\partial}{\partial z} \left[ K \frac {\partial v}{\partial z} \right] + \frac {A}{T _ {0}} = 0, \tag {3}
\left. \begin{array}{l} v = 0 \text {a t} z = 0, h \text {f o r} 0 \leq r \leq a \\ v = 0 \text {a t} r = a \text {f o r} 0 \leq z \leq h \end{array} \right\} \tag {4}
\Theta (r, z) = \frac {1}{K _ {0}} \int_ {0} ^ {v _ {s}} K d v, \tag {5}
\frac {\partial \Theta}{\partial r} = \frac {K}{K _ {o}} \frac {\partial v}{\partial r}; \frac {\partial \Theta}{\partial z} = \frac {K}{K _ {o}} \frac {\partial v}{\partial z}. \tag {6}

\frac {1}{r} \frac {\partial}{\partial r} \left[ r \frac {\partial \Theta}{\partial r} \right] + \frac {\partial}{\partial z} \left[ \frac {\partial \Theta}{\partial z} \right] + \frac {A}{K _ {o} T _ {o}} = 0. \tag {7}
\left. \begin{array}{l} ⑧ = 0 \text {a t} z = 0, h \text {f o r} 0 \leq r \leq a \\ ⑨ = 0 \text {a t} r = a \text {f o r} 0 \leq z \leq h \end{array} \right\}. \tag {8}
x \equiv r / a, \quad z \equiv z / a, \quad l \equiv h / a. \tag {9}
\Theta = A a ^ {2} U / K _ {\circ} T _ {\circ}. \tag {11}
\Theta = \frac {1}{K _ {\circ} T _ {\circ}} \int_ {T _ {\circ}} ^ {T} K d T. \tag {12}
(10) and (11) and then evaluate $v$ and $T$ from Eqs.
(5) in order to calculate a table of $\Theta$ as a function of $v$ .
When thermal conductivity is a linear function of temperature, we require two thermal conductivities at two temperatures, $(T_{1}, K_{1})$ and $(T_{2}, K_{2})$ , from which we obtain
K = K _ {\circ} \left[ 1 + b \left(T - T _ {\circ}\right) \right], \tag {13}
K _ {o} = \frac {K _ {1} \left(T _ {2} - T _ {o}\right) - K _ {2} \left(T _ {1} - T _ {o}\right)}{\left(T _ {2} - T _ {1}\right)},
\Theta = \frac {1}{K _ {o}} \int_ {o} ^ {v} K d v = \int_ {o} ^ {v} (1 + b T _ {o} v) d v = v + \frac {b T _ {o} v ^ {2}}{2}. \tag {14}
In this case, the value of $\mathbb{O}$ is known and $v$ and $T - T_{0}$ can be calculated from the relationships
v = \left[ \sqrt {1 + 2 b T _ {o}} - 1 \right] / b T _ {o} \tag {15}
\Theta (x, z) = \frac {\left(T - T _ {0}\right)}{T _ {0} K _ {0}} \left(\frac {K + K _ {0}}{2}\right) = \frac {\bar {K} \Delta T}{T _ {0} K _ {0}}, \tag {17}
\bar {K} \equiv \frac {K + K _ {0}}{2} \tag {18}
A = \frac {\overline {{K}} \Delta T}{\alpha^ {2} U}. \tag {20}
A = \frac {4 \overline {{K}} \Delta T}{a ^ {2}}. \tag {21}

Equation (21), while strictly applicable only to an infinite cylinder, is frequently used and yields a rather accurate estimate of the specific power, $A$ , as a function of $\Delta T$ , providing that the cylinder is long (i.e., a cylinder with a length/diameter ratio in excess of about 3 or 4).
The quantity $U$ , calculated in program STORE, has values of 0.201, 0.245, 0.249, and 0.250 for cylinders of length/diameter ratios of 1, 2, 2.5, and 5, respectively.
(21) overestimates the $\Delta T$ for a given value of $A$ , but to a significant extent only if the length/diameter ratio is less than 2.
(21), we note that, at the center of a cylinder, namely at $x = 0$ and $Z = \frac{L}{2}$ , Eq.

Thus, for $a / h \ll 1$ , we would obtain Eq.
In this case, we solve for $(T - T_{0})$ by the procedure
\frac {T - T _ {o}}{T _ {o}} = \Theta = A a ^ {2} U / K _ {o} T _ {o}. \tag {25}
A reasonable number is in the range 20 to 45.
We have used as many as 100 and as few as 5; in general, a value in the range 10 to 20 will be adequate.

Thus, whether the radius of the cylinder is subsequently set to 0.1, 5.0, 25.0, or $100.0\mathrm{cm}$ , all the calculations will be performed at the centerline and, for example, $5\%$ , $10\%$ , etc., out toward the surface.
One temperature, TP(NT), and its thermal conductivity, TK(NT), are punched on a card, at $50^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ intervals as the program is now written.
DIMENSION allows a maximum of 99 such pairs of values.
At the end of the table, any negative floating-point number, such as $-1.0$ , is entered in the TP(NT) field (columns 1 - 10) to indicate the end of the table.
The temperature interval $50.0^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ was chosen because it is appropriate for materials heated to temperatures in the range 0.0 to $3000^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ or higher.

READ 9021, A, RAD, TINT, TO
A is the internal (absorbed) power density in cal sec $^{-1}$ cm $^{-3}$ , as in Eq.
Values in the range 0.1 to 0.8 are significant in connection with high-level radioactive wastes from nuclear fuel processing.

For example, if the temperature difference $(\mathbf{T} - \mathbf{T}_0)$ is expected to be on the order of $100^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , then TINT might be assumed to be 5. or 10., corresponding to the radial and vertical locations of temperatures spaced 5 or $10^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ apart.
TK0 is the thermal conductivity of the material at $T_{0}$ , cal cm $^{-1}$ sec $^{-1}$ oC $^{-1}$ .

Each of the three programs (see Appendix A), namely, STORE, TKLIN (when K is a linear function of T), and TKCON (when K is constant), has been executed many times on the IBM/360-75 at ORNL to calculate temperature profiles in cylinders containing high- or intermediate-level radioactive wastes generated by the processing of nuclear fuels.
Typically, the programs require 30 to 40 sec for compilation.
Depending primarily on the number of temperatures at which profile data are desired, TKCON and TKLIN require 1 to 10 sec per case.
Program STORE has a longer execution time, again depending (but less strongly) on the number of temperatures at which profile values are required; it generally requires 5 to 30 sec per case.

(1), based on the use of finite-difference equations, a grid of 30 radial divisions, 120 vertical divisions, and the near-minimum of 500 iterations, requires on the order of 5 min with FORTRAN IV, level H execution under optimum timing.
Output from STORE includes a table (Table 1) of NZ times NR values of U [Eq.
(10)], a table (Table 2) of temperatures above the surface temperature, $(T - T_{\circ})$ , and a table of $\Theta$ values.

Output also includes a table (Table 3) of distances, in radius units, measured from the bottom or the top of the cylinder, where heating above the surface temperature occurs by multiples of the quantity TINT.
The maximum number of such iterations in the studies reported here was 20; usually, however, this number did not exceed 2.
